data.title: Corcept Therapeutics (CORT) Is Up 13.0% After FDA Approves Lifyorli Combo in Resistant Ovarian Cancer
data.description:
• In March 2026, Corcept Therapeutics received FDA approval for Lifyorli (relacorilant) plus nab-paclitaxel to treat adults with platinum-resistant ovarian, fallopian tube, or primary peritoneal cancer after one to three prior regimens including bevacizumab.
  • This approval, grounded in the ROSELLA trial’s survival benefit and establishing Lifyorli as the first FDA-approved selective glucocorticoid receptor antagonist, materially expands Corcept’s oncology footprint and validates its cortisol-modulation approach.
  • We’ll now examine how FDA approval of Lifyorli in platinum-resistant ovarian cancer reshapes Corcept’s investment narrative and risk-reward profile.

Corcept Therapeutics Investment Narrative Recap

To own Corcept today, you need to believe that cortisol modulation can support a durable, multi-drug franchise that gradually reduces dependence on Korlym while absorbing higher R&D and SG&A. FDA approval of Lifyorli in platinum-resistant ovarian cancer strengthens that case and, in the near term, meaningfully reduces the regulatory overhang around relacorilant, though litigation over past disclosures and Korlym’s patent exposure remain, in my view, the most important risks to watch.

The most relevant recent development alongside Lifyorli’s approval is the flurry of securities class action filings tied to the FDA’s December 2025 Complete Response Letter for relacorilant in hypercortisolism. That legal overhang directly contrasts with the oncology win and could influence how the market discounts future regulatory milestones, even if it does not affect Lifyorli’s new ovarian cancer label or near term launch activities.

data.title: A Look At Sable Offshore’s Valuation As Santa Ynez Restart And Oil Sales Gain Traction
data.description:
Sable Offshore (SOC) has restarted the Santa Ynez Pipeline System under a Defense Production Act order, with oil sales from the Santa Ynez Unit now underway. This development is reshaping how investors view its production capacity and risk profile.

See our latest analysis for Sable Offshore.

Despite the latest 4.56% 1 day share price return to US$15.37, the recent Santa Ynez restart and analyst commentary have been met with a 16.69% 7 day share price pullback. The 30 day share price return of 10.97% and 90 day gain of 31.03% suggest momentum has cooled in the short term but remains stronger over a multi month window, and the 1 year total shareholder return of a 16.65% decline contrasts with a 51.73% gain over three years.

If this kind of event driven move has your attention, it can be worth seeing what other energy names are setting up for their own inflection points through 28 power grid technology and infrastructure stocks

With the stock at US$15.37, a market cap of about US$2.26b, analyst targets near US$26.80 and fresh Santa Ynez volumes coming online, is there still mispricing here, or are markets already baking in future growth?

Advertisement

Preferred Price to Book of 4.2x: Is it justified?

Sable Offshore currently trades on a P/B of 4.2x, which sets a high bar for a business that is still loss making and coming off minimal reported revenue.

P/B compares the company’s market value to its book value, effectively asking how many dollars investors are paying for each dollar of net assets. For an offshore oil and gas operator with producing platforms and pipelines, this ratio gives a quick read on how the market is pricing those hard assets relative to their accounting value.

Here, the 4.2x P/B is meaningfully higher than both the US Oil and Gas industry average of 1.7x and the closer peer group average of 1.3x. That kind of premium suggests investors are already assigning considerable value to Santa Ynez restart volumes and the forecast revenue and earnings ramp, even though the company currently reports a net loss of $410.162m and has a value score of 1 out of 6.

Against that backdrop, the gap between Sable Offshore’s 4.2x P/B and lower industry and peer averages indicates the market is pricing in a much richer future than the sector norm, rather than treating the stock as a balance sheet value story.
